data.text: Let me start by reading the plan file referenced in the bead description.

data.output:
1	# Visual Suite — Principles, Tools, User Workflow
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Draft v6 (locked; reviewers' Q1–Q6 resolved)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	DDx ships a coordinated graphical suite covering three lenses on the product:
9	**principles**, **tools**, and **user workflow**. Each lens gets per-element
10	prompts plus a composite; the user-workflow lens is the capstone.
11	
12	Visual style is established via Google's **DESIGN.md** format spec
13	(github.com/google-labs-code/design.md) — markdown + YAML frontmatter
14	describing tokens (colors, typography, spacing, components) plus prose
15	rationale. Comes with a `@google/design.md` npm CLI for `lint`, `diff`,
16	`export` (Tailwind/DTCG), and `spec`. **Status: alpha, version `0.1.0`.**
17	
18	Project standardizes on **bun/bunx** for Node-ecosystem tooling, matching
19	existing precedent (the SvelteKit frontend at `cli/internal/server/frontend/`
20	already uses Bun per CLAUDE.md). `package.json` is the canonical declaration
21	surface for npm-delivered tools — no DDx wrapper, no FEAT-024 spec, no
22	DDx-owned lockfile.
23	
24	All visuals are generated artifacts (per the artifact-and-run-architecture
25	plan), live in the helix phase tree alongside the docs they depict, with
26	sidecar metadata and `generated_by` provenance.
27	
28	## Architecture
29	
30	```
31	DESIGN.md authored at repo root
32	      ↓
33	DESIGN.md → website palette via Hextra theme overrides (spike — see V3.5)
34	DESIGN.md tokens → image-generation prompts (palette anchors)
35	      ↓
36	Visual generation (one at a time, $10 cap, pressure-test gated)
37	      ↓
38	┌────────────────────────────────┐
39	│ Principle prompts (×6)         │
40	│   ↓                            │
41	│ Principle composite (capstone) │
42	├────────────────────────────────┤
43	│ Tool prompts (×4)              │
44	│   ↓                            │
45	│ Tool graphics + composite      │
46	├────────────────────────────────┤
47	│ User-workflow diagram (capstone)
48	└────────────────────────────────┘
49	      ↓
50	Website redesign (beads to be created — 28, 29) consumes
51	```
52	
53	## The three lenses
54	
55	### Lens 1 — Principles (×6 prompts; composite ships; standalone optional)
56	
57	One prompt per thesis principle. Lever/load/fulcrum metaphor is load-bearing.
58	
59	| Principle | Visual concept |
60	|---|---|
61	| 1. Abstraction is the lever | The lever arm — multi-level stack with intent transmitting |
62	| 2. Software is iteration over tracked work | Cyclic motion of the lever — discrete tracked items |
63	| 3. Methodology is plural | Multiple lever handles / interchangeable grips |
64	| 4. LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, costly | The load — non-uniform, probabilistic mass |
65	| 5. Evidence provides memory | Trail / chain of receipts left by the moving lever |
66	| 6. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum | The pivot — humans and AI in contact at the seam |
67	
68	All 6 prompts authored regardless of render decision. Composite ships first
69	(pressure-test the metaphor); standalone renders are optional follow-up if
70	the composite reads well alone.
71	
72	### Lens 2 — Tools (×4 + composite — full per-element + composite)
73	
74	| Tool | Visual concept |
75	|---|---|
76	| Bead tracker | DAG with priority queue + ready/blocked states |
77	| Document graph (artifacts) | Multi-node graph with `depends_on` and `generated_by` edges |
78	| Agentic execution (run/try/work) | Three-layer wrapping; worktree isolation visible |
79	| Plugins | Modular composition; HELIX/Dun snapping into shared core |
80	
81	### Lens 3 — User workflow (capstone)
82	
83	Iterative diagram showing how a user actually works with DDx over time:
84	author/refine artifact → graph synthesizes context → bead created → agent
85	runs (`ddx try` / `ddx work`) → evidence captured → human re-aligns → loop
86	closes. Explanatory register; load-bearing artifact.
87	
88	## Storage — helix phase tree
89	
90	Visuals live alongside the docs they depict. DESIGN.md is the cross-cutting
91	exception at repo root.
92	
93	```
94	DESIGN.md                                          ← cross-cutting; repo root
95	package.json                                       ← repo root (bun-managed)
96	bun.lock                                           ← repo root (text lockfile)
97	docs/helix/00-discover/
98	├── product-vision.md
99	└── visuals/
100	    ├── principle-1-lever.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
101	    ├── ... (×6 principles)
102	    └── principles-composite.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
103	docs/helix/01-frame/
104	├── prd.md
105	└── visuals/
106	    ├── tool-tracker.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
107	    ├── tool-doc-graph.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
108	    ├── tool-execution.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
109	    ├── tool-plugins.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
110	    ├── tools-composite.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
111	    └── user-workflow.{prompt.md,png,png.ddx.yaml}
112	```
113	
114	Each visual artifact has `depends_on` pointing to the source doc and
115	`generated_by` pointing to its prompt source. First real test of
116	`generated_by` edges across the helix tree.
117	
118	## DESIGN.md is the visual style brief
119	
120	DESIGN.md *is* the style brief — its prose sections (Overview, Do's/Don'ts)
121	carry metaphor system and composition rules; its YAML carries tokens. No
122	separate STYLE.md needed.
123	
124	Two consumers:
125	- **Website (Hugo + Hextra):** spike palette injection via Hextra's
126	  `params.theme` / CSS variables (V3.5). Hextra ships precompiled CSS via
127	  Hugo Pipes; project-level Tailwind config consumption is unverified.
128	  Fall back to accept-divergence (DESIGN.md drives prompts only) if
129	  Hextra reach is insufficient.
130	- **Image prompts:** prompts reference DESIGN.md palette by hex and named
131	  tokens for cross-prompt consistency anchor.
132	
133	Repo placement: `/DESIGN.md` (brand-level artifact for the whole product).
134	
135	## Tool dependency declaration: `package.json` (no DDx wrapper)
136	
137	Standard Node convention. No FEAT-024 spec, no DDx-owned lockfile, no
138	`ddx tool run` wrapper.
139	
140	```bash
141	bun add --dev @google/design.md@0.1.0      # adds to package.json + bun.lock
142	bunx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md      # standard invocation
143	```
144	
145	`ddx doctor` extension scans known `package.json` locations (root,
146	`website/`, `cli/internal/server/frontend/`) and reports `bun install` if
147	`node_modules/` is missing or out of date. `ddx init` summary mentions
148	discovered `package.json` files.
149	
150	DDx never wraps `bunx`; users invoke npm-ecosystem tools directly via
151	standard `bunx <pkg>` commands.
152	
153	## Generation strategy
154	
155	- **Generator:** `nano-banana-pro-openrouter` (Gemini 3 Pro Image preview)
156	  by default.
157	- **Each visual is a `generate-artifact` run** per the artifact-and-run plan.
158	- **`SYSTEM_TEMPLATE` override** for sober/utilitarian style (default
159	  pulls toward "dreamy illustration").
160	- **DESIGN.md tokens injected** into every prompt for palette/typography
161	  consistency.
162	- **Sequential rendering with $10 total budget cap and $1.50/image halt
163	  trigger.** Render one visual at a time; evaluate against acceptance
164	  criteria before generating the next. Halt on either cap. Per-image
165	  cap prevents one runaway prompt eating the budget.
166	- **First real dogfood** of the artifact infrastructure (sidecar I/O,
167	  `generated_by` edges, `ddx artifact regenerate` CLI). Bugs surfaced here
168	  feed back into beads `ddx-d5e71fb3` (FEAT-010), `ddx-5d92b873` (FEAT-007),
169	  and the artifact-regenerate implementation.
170	- **Regen policy:** every visual `depends_on` DESIGN.md and its prompt
171	  source. Either-source change marks the visual stale via the separate
172	  `generated_by` staleness rule. Regen is explicit (`ddx artifact regenerate`),
173	  not automatic — operator decides when to spend tokens.
174	
175	## Acceptance criteria per visual
176	
177	- Renders cleanly at intended placement (homepage hero, docs page, README)
178	- Contrast OK in light and dark mode
179	- Mobile crop preserves the message
180	- Alt text drafted and in sidecar
181	- File size budget under 200KB (compress / downsize as needed)
182	- Metaphor parses without explanatory copy
183	- **Brand-fit: sober/utilitarian register matching DDx's docs surface; no
184	  AI-gloss, no kitsch, no dreamy illustration**
185	
186	## Risks
187	
188	- **DESIGN.md is alpha** (`0.1.0`). Pin exact `0.1.0`; budget for both
189	  format spec AND `--format tailwind` exporter breaking on bumps (two
190	  alpha surfaces stack).
191	- **Hextra Tailwind path unverified.** V3.5 is a spike. Expected outcome:
192	  palette override via Hextra theme params works; if not, fall back to
193	  accept-divergence rather than fork Hextra.
194	- **Cross-prompt style consistency** on `gemini-3-pro-image-preview` is
195	  unproven. DESIGN.md tokens anchor palette but composition consistency
196	  across 6+ subsections is genuine risk. Composite-first sequencing
197	  (V6 before V7) limits exposure.
198	- **Metaphor strain when rendered.** Pressure-test in V6 before committing
199	  further. If lever/fulcrum/load doesn't carry, revise the visual concept
200	  (not principle wording).
201	- **Artifact-infra bugs** could block visual generation. Plan accepts
202	  iteration: bugs → fixes in artifact-and-run beads → resume.
203	- **Cost overrun.** $10 cap is for the full run; halt at cap and reassess.
204	  Sequential generation gives visibility; cache-on-prompt-hash prevents
205	  accidental re-spending.
206	- **Two alpha surfaces.** DESIGN.md format and exporter both alpha.
207	  Pinning `0.1.0` exactly mitigates but doesn't eliminate risk; budget
208	  ~half a day per bump for migration.
209	
210	## Bead breakdown
211	
212	### Tooling foundation (gates the rest)
213	
214	- **V2.** Extend `ddx doctor` (and optionally `ddx init` summary) to detect
215	  `package.json` at known locations (root, `website/`, `cli/internal/server/frontend/`)
216	  and report `bun install` if `node_modules/` is missing or stale. ~100 LoC.
217	
218	### DESIGN.md integration
219	
220	- **V3.** Author `DESIGN.md` at repo root. Capture existing precedent
221	  (logo, current rgba triad, terminal-demo tone). Tokens (colors,
222	  typography, spacing, components); prose Overview, Do's/Don'ts, metaphor
223	  system. Run `bun add --dev @google/design.md@0.1.0` at repo root; lint
224	  with `bunx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md`. **Treat as foundation,
225	  not throwaway spike** — DDx has no visual language today; embedding
226	  DESIGN.md is correct. Adapt as the format evolves.
227	- **V3.5.** Wire DESIGN.md → website (Hugo + Hextra) palette. Try Hextra
228	  `params.theme` / CSS-variable override path first. Fall back to
229	  accept-divergence (DESIGN.md drives image prompts only) if Hextra
230	  reach insufficient. Document outcome in bead resolution.
231	- **V3.6.** Wire DESIGN.md → frontend (SvelteKit + Tailwind v4) at
232	  `cli/internal/server/frontend/tailwind.config.js`. Use
233	  `bunx @google/design.md export --format tailwind` to produce a
234	  theme JSON the frontend's tailwind.config.js consumes. Easier path
235	  than Hextra (frontend already uses Tailwind); ensures DESIGN.md ROI
236	  even if V3.5 falls back to accept-divergence. **Goal: align on a new
237	  visual language across the entire product surface.**
238	- **V4.** Lefthook + CI lint integration: `bunx @google/design.md lint
239	  DESIGN.md` runs on commit if DESIGN.md is modified.
240	
241	### Principles lens
242	
243	- **V5.** Author all 6 principle-graphic prompts at
244	  `docs/helix/00-discover/visuals/principle-N-*.prompt.md`.
245	- **V6.** Author principle-composite prompt + generate **(first render —
246	  pressure-test gate; budget tracking starts here)**. If the lever/fulcrum/
247	  load metaphor doesn't render usefully, pause and revise before proceeding.
248	  **Hard-blocked on artifact infrastructure**: beads `ddx-d5e71fb3` (FEAT-010
249	  three-layer substrate), `ddx-5d92b873` (FEAT-007 sidecar + generated_by),
250	  and `ddx artifact regenerate` CLI implementation must be merged first.
251	  No manual prompt-file → image fallback; if those slip, V6 slips.
252	- **V7** *(optional, conditional on V6 outcome).* Render the 6 principle
253	  subsections standalone. Skip if composite suffices or if cross-prompt
254	  consistency fails.
255	
256	### Tools lens
257	
258	- **V8.** Author 4 tool-graphic prompts at `docs/helix/01-frame/visuals/`.
259	- **V9.** Generate 4 tool graphics + tool composite **(sequential, budget-tracked)**.
260	
261	### Workflow capstone
262	
263	- **V10.** Author user-workflow prompt + generate **(sequential,
264	  budget-tracked)**. Explanatory register; load-bearing.
265	
266	### Website integration (downstream — beads not yet created)
267	
268	- Replace 6 feature cards on landing with 6 principle cards.
269	- Add Why DDx top-nav page (exposition around principles).
270	- Concept "Why this exists" callouts.
271	- Replace README tagline with thesis.
272	- E2E test updates.
273	- Visual integration consumes V6/V9/V10 outputs.
274	
275	## Reviewer ask
276	
277	1. **Tool-declaration simplification** — `package.json` + `ddx doctor`
278	   detection only, no FEAT-024, no DDx wrapper. Right reduction, or did we
279	   lose something load-bearing?
280	2. **bun standardization** — repo already uses Bun for the frontend per
281	   CLAUDE.md. Any subdirectory or build step that breaks if root tooling
282	   moves to bun (e.g., CI assumed npm)?
283	3. **Multiple `package.json` scan in `ddx doctor`** — known set (root,
284	   `website/`, `cli/internal/server/frontend/`) vs glob discovery? Static
285	   list is simpler; glob risks scanning user output dirs.
286	4. **`package.json` placement for `@google/design.md`** — root chosen for
287	   cross-cutting linter. Concerns about polluting repo root with a
288	   `package.json`/`node_modules` when most of the project is Go?
289	5. **Hextra spike fallback (accept-divergence)** — if Hextra can't consume
290	   DESIGN.md tokens, website CSS stays Hextra-default and DESIGN.md drives
291	   image prompts only. Is image-prompt-only consumption sufficient ROI to
292	   justify the DESIGN.md effort?
293	6. **Cost cap $10 sequential** — realistic for 11+ Gemini-3-Pro-Image
294	   generations at 2K? Should we set a per-image cost ceiling too?
295	7. **Acceptance criteria** — anything missing? Brand-fit / kitsch judgment?
296	8. **Alpha-stack risk** — two alpha surfaces (DESIGN.md format + exporter).
297	   Sufficient to pin `0.1.0`, or should we wait until v1?
298	9. **Helix-tree storage of visuals** — `docs/helix/00-discover/visuals/`
299	   and `docs/helix/01-frame/visuals/`. Anti-patterns or convention violations?
300	10. **Anything missing.**
301	
302	Codebase context:
303	- `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md`
304	- `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/CLAUDE.md` (bun precedent)
305	- `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/website/` (Hugo + Hextra; no Tailwind today)
306	- `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/server/frontend/` (existing bun/SvelteKit setup)
307	- `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.agents/skills/nano-banana-pro-openrouter/`
308	- DESIGN.md spec: github.com/google-labs-code/design.md
309	
310	Respond with sections (omit empty):
311	**Keep:** correct, load-bearing
312	**Cut:** remove
313	**Missing:** gaps
314	**Risks:** verification needed (cite file:line)
315	**Questions:** require USER input
316	
317	Concise. 2 sentences per finding. Severity-ordered.
318

baseURL: https://DocumentDrivenDX.github.io/ddx/
languageCode: en-us
title: DDx — Document-Driven Development

module:
  imports:
    - path: github.com/imfing/hextra

enableRobotsTXT: true
enableGitInfo: true

markup:
  goldmark:
    renderer:
      unsafe: true
  highlight:
    noClasses: false

menu:
  main:
    - name: Docs
      pageRef: /docs
      weight: 1
    - name: Concepts
      pageRef: /docs/concepts
      weight: 2
    - name: CLI Reference
      pageRef: /docs/cli
      weight: 3
    - name: Skills
      pageRef: /docs/skills
      weight: 4
    - name: Plugins
      pageRef: /docs/plugins
      weight: 5
    - name: GitHub
      url: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx
      weight: 10
      params:
        icon: github

params:
  description: Tools for maintaining the documents that drive AI agents to build software.

  navbar:
    displayTitle: true
    displayLogo: false

  page:
    width: wide

data.text: The package installed successfully. Now let me understand the DESIGN.md format by checking the installed package.

data.output:
dist
LICENSE
package.json
README.md
# DESIGN.md

A format specification for describing a visual identity to coding agents. DESIGN.md gives agents a persistent, structured understanding of a design system.

## The Format

A DESIGN.md file combines machine-readable design tokens (YAML front matter) with human-readable design rationale (markdown prose). Tokens give agents exact values. Prose tells them *why* those values exist and how to apply them.

```md
---
name: Heritage
colors:
  primary: "#1A1C1E"
  secondary: "#6C7278"
  tertiary: "#B8422E"
  neutral: "#F7F5F2"
typography:
  h1:
    fontFamily: Public Sans
    fontSize: 3rem
  body-md:
    fontFamily: Public Sans
    fontSize: 1rem
  label-caps:
    fontFamily: Space Grotesk
    fontSize: 0.75rem
rounded:
  sm: 4px
  md: 8px
spacing:
  sm: 8px
  md: 16px
---

## Overview

Architectural Minimalism meets Journalistic Gravitas. The UI evokes a
premium matte finish — a high-end broadsheet or contemporary gallery.

## Colors

The palette is rooted in high-contrast neutrals and a single accent color.

- **Primary (#1A1C1E):** Deep ink for headlines and core text.
- **Secondary (#6C7278):** Sophisticated slate for borders, captions, metadata.
- **Tertiary (#B8422E):** "Boston Clay" — the sole driver for interaction.
- **Neutral (#F7F5F2):** Warm limestone foundation, softer than pure white.
```

An agent that reads this file will produce a UI with deep ink headlines in Public Sans, a warm limestone background, and Boston Clay call-to-action buttons.

## Getting Started

Validate a DESIGN.md against the spec, catch broken token references, check WCAG contrast ratios, and surface structural findings — all as structured JSON that agents can act on.

```bash
npx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md
```

```json
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "path": "components.button-primary",
      "message": "textColor (#ffffff) on backgroundColor (#1A1C1E) has contrast ratio 15.42:1 — passes WCAG AA."
    }
  ],
  "summary": { "errors": 0, "warnings": 1, "info": 1 }
}
```

Compare two versions of a design system to detect token-level and prose regressions:

```bash
npx @google/design.md diff DESIGN.md DESIGN-v2.md
```

```json
{
  "tokens": {
    "colors": { "added": ["accent"], "removed": [], "modified": ["tertiary"] },
    "typography": { "added": [], "removed": [], "modified": [] }
  },
  "regression": false
}
```

## The Specification

The full DESIGN.md spec lives at [`docs/spec.md`](docs/spec.md). What follows is a condensed reference.

### File Structure

A DESIGN.md file has two layers:

1. **YAML front matter** — Machine-readable design tokens, delimited by `---` fences at the top of the file.
2. **Markdown body** — Human-readable design rationale organized into `##` sections.

The tokens are the normative values. The prose provides context for how to apply them.

### Token Schema

```yaml
version: <string>          # optional, current: "alpha"
name: <string>
description: <string>      # optional
colors:
  <token-name>: <Color>
typography:
  <token-name>: <Typography>
rounded:
  <scale-level>: <Dimension>
spacing:
  <scale-level>: <Dimension | number>
components:
  <component-name>:
    <token-name>: <string | token reference>
```

### Token Types

| Type | Format | Example |
|:-----|:-------|:--------|
| Color | `#` + hex (sRGB) | `"#1A1C1E"` |
| Dimension | number + unit (`px`, `em`, `rem`) | `48px`, `-0.02em` |
| Token Reference | `{path.to.token}` | `{colors.primary}` |
| Typography | object with `fontFamily`, `fontSize`, `fontWeight`, `lineHeight`, `letterSpacing`, `fontFeature`, `fontVariation` | See example above |

### Section Order

Sections use `##` headings. They can be omitted, but those present must appear in this order:

| # | Section | Aliases |
|:--|:--------|:--------|
| 1 | Overview | Brand & Style |
| 2 | Colors | |
| 3 | Typography | |
| 4 | Layout | Layout & Spacing |
| 5 | Elevation & Depth | Elevation |
| 6 | Shapes | |
| 7 | Components | |
| 8 | Do's and Don'ts | |

### Component Tokens

Components map a name to a group of sub-token properties:

```yaml
components:
  button-primary:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.tertiary}"
    textColor: "{colors.on-tertiary}"
    rounded: "{rounded.sm}"
    padding: 12px
  button-primary-hover:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.tertiary-container}"
```

Valid component properties: `backgroundColor`, `textColor`, `typography`, `rounded`, `padding`, `size`, `height`, `width`.

Variants (hover, active, pressed) are expressed as separate component entries with a related key name.

### Consumer Behavior for Unknown Content

| Scenario | Behavior |
|:---------|:---------|
| Unknown section heading | Preserve; do not error |
| Unknown color token name | Accept if value is valid |
| Unknown typography token name | Accept as valid typography |
| Unknown component property | Accept with warning |
| Duplicate section heading | Error; reject the file |

## CLI Reference

### Installation

```bash
npm install @google/design.md
```

Or run directly:

```bash
npx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md
```

All commands accept a file path or `-` for stdin. Output defaults to JSON.

### `lint`

Validate a DESIGN.md file for structural correctness.

```bash
npx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md
npx @google/design.md lint --format json DESIGN.md
cat DESIGN.md | npx @google/design.md lint -
```

| Option | Type | Default | Description |

data.text: Now I have enough context. Let me write the DESIGN.md file.

input.content:
---
version: "alpha"
name: DDx
description: Visual language for Document-Driven Development eXperience — a terminal-first developer tool whose three-color identity maps to the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor.
colors:
  primary: "#4878C6"
  secondary: "#35A35F"
  tertiary: "#8E35A3"
  background: "#FFFFFF"
  surface: "#F9FAFB"
  surface-dark: "#0F1117"
  text: "#111827"
  text-muted: "#6B7280"
  text-on-primary: "#FFFFFF"
  text-on-secondary: "#FFFFFF"
  text-on-tertiary: "#FFFFFF"
  border: "#E5E7EB"
  terminal-bg: "#0F1117"
  terminal-fg: "#E8EAF0"
typography:
  h1:
    fontFamily: Inter
    fontSize: 2.5rem
    fontWeight: 700
    lineHeight: 1.2
  h2:
    fontFamily: Inter
    fontSize: 1.75rem
    fontWeight: 600
    lineHeight: 1.3
  body-md:
    fontFamily: Inter
    fontSize: 1rem
    fontWeight: 400
    lineHeight: 1.6
  body-sm:
    fontFamily: Inter
    fontSize: 0.875rem
    fontWeight: 400
    lineHeight: 1.5
  code:
    fontFamily: JetBrains Mono
    fontSize: 0.875rem
    fontWeight: 400
    lineHeight: 1.5
  label:
    fontFamily: Inter
    fontSize: 0.75rem
    fontWeight: 500
    lineHeight: 1.4
    letterSpacing: 0.05em
rounded:
  sm: 4px
  md: 8px
  lg: 12px
spacing:
  xs: 4px
  sm: 8px
  md: 16px
  lg: 24px
  xl: 40px
components:
  button-primary:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.primary}"
    textColor: "{colors.text-on-primary}"
    rounded: "{rounded.md}"
    padding: 10px
  button-alt:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.surface}"
    textColor: "{colors.text}"
    rounded: "{rounded.md}"
    padding: 10px
  feature-card:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.background}"
    textColor: "{colors.text}"
    rounded: "{rounded.lg}"
    padding: 24px
  terminal-block:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.terminal-bg}"
    textColor: "{colors.terminal-fg}"
    rounded: "{rounded.md}"
    padding: 16px
---

## Overview

DDx is a terminal-first developer platform. Its visual language is **sober, utilitarian, and purposely non-decorative** — the antithesis of AI-gloss, dreamy illustration, and gradient excess. Every visual choice serves comprehension, not style.

The product's three-color identity maps directly to its core metaphor: the **lever, the load, and the fulcrum**. Abstraction is the lever — it transmits intent across levels of the stack. LLMs are the load — probabilistic, costly, not fully in control. Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum — the stable pivot where judgment meets execution. These are not decorative categories; they are architectural facts about how DDx works, and the palette encodes them.

The primary surface is documentation — markdown, terminal output, CLI reference pages. Visuals live alongside text, not in competition with it. The register is developer-grade: precise, legible at a glance, trustworthy at scale.

## Colors

Three brand colors anchor the palette, each carrying semantic weight from the metaphor system.

- **Primary (#4878C6 — lever blue):** The mechanism. Used for primary actions, links, dispatch signals, and any UI element that transmits intent — the lever arm in motion.
- **Secondary (#35A35F — fulcrum green):** The pivot. Used for success states, progress indicators, and human-AI collaboration surfaces — stable ground where control is exercised.
- **Tertiary (#8E35A3 — load purple):** The weight. Used for AI/agent surfaces, stochastic outputs, cost indicators, and any context where probabilistic or uncertain mass is present.

These three colors appear as radial gradients on feature cards on the landing page (15% opacity on white), preserving legibility while anchoring semantic meaning. At full opacity they are used only for icons, badges, and deliberate accent marks — never as large fill blocks.

Surface and text tokens support both light-mode documentation and dark terminal contexts. The terminal palette (`terminal-bg: #0F1117`, `terminal-fg: #E8EAF0`) matches the tone of the animated demo recordings that are the product's primary visual artifact.

## Typography

Two families. One for prose and UI; one for code.

- **Inter** handles all headings, body copy, labels, and navigation. It is neutral without being generic: the right weight for technical documentation that needs to be read fast, not admired.
- **JetBrains Mono** handles all code blocks, terminal output, CLI references, and inline code. Monospace is not decorative here — it is the medium. Most of what DDx produces and consumes is text in a terminal.

Type scale is conservative: three heading sizes, two body sizes, one label size. No display sizes. This is reference material, not marketing copy.

## Layout

Wide-page layout, no max-width on docs pages. Content takes the space it needs; sidebars carry navigation, not decoration. Spacing scale is small-grained at the low end (4px grid) to support tight CLI output tables and artifact metadata blocks.

Horizontal rhythm: content rarely bleeds to the viewport edge. Comfortable line lengths (65–80 characters) for prose sections; full width for tables and code blocks.

## Components

**button-primary:** The primary call-to-action — lever blue with white text. Used for "Get Started" and primary agent-dispatch triggers. Square-ish radius (8px), not pill. Pill buttons signal consumer software; DDx is a developer tool.

**button-alt:** Secondary action — light surface with dark text. Used alongside button-primary for "Learn More" and non-destructive secondary choices.

**feature-card:** White card with 12px radius, 24px interior padding. Background accent is a radial gradient at 15% opacity using the appropriate brand color (blue for lever/tracker, green for fulcrum/plugins, purple for load/execution). Never a full-opacity color fill.

**terminal-block:** Dark (#0F1117) block with light text (#E8EAF0), 8px radius, 16px padding. Used for CLI command examples, agent output, and demo code. This is the product's native context; treat it as first-class, not supplementary.

## Do's and Don'ts

**Do:**
- Use the three brand colors with semantic intent — blue for action/dispatch, green for collaboration/success, purple for AI/stochastic contexts.
- Maintain the sober, utilitarian register. If an image could appear on an AI company's landing page, it does not belong here.
- Render the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor concretely: physical objects, mechanical diagrams, DAG graphs, state machines. Abstract geometry is acceptable only if the metaphor parses without explanatory copy.
- Use dark backgrounds for terminal-context visuals. The animated demo recordings set the tone; generated images should match it.
- Keep file sizes under 200KB for generated visuals. Compress and downsize before committing.

**Don't:**
- Use gradient washes, glows, lens flares, particle effects, or any visual treatment that signals "AI art." This is a developer tool, not a product launch deck.
- Use the three brand colors interchangeably. Each color carries a specific role in the metaphor system; mixing them dilutes the signal.
- Place the lever/load/fulcrum metaphor as decorative background texture. If it appears, it carries meaning.
- Use illustration styles that anthropomorphize the AI components (no robots, no glowing brains, no circuit-board heads). LLMs are load — treat them as mass, not character.
- Ship visuals that require explanatory copy to parse. If the metaphor doesn't read in the image, revise the visual concept, not the caption.

## Metaphor System

The lever/load/fulcrum triad is the load-bearing metaphor for all visual communication across the three lenses (principles, tools, user workflow).

| Element | Role | Visual treatment |
|---|---|---|
| Lever | Abstraction transmitting intent across levels | Arm, mechanism, directional force, stack layers |
| Load | LLM output — probabilistic, costly, non-uniform | Mass, weight, irregular shape, probabilistic scatter |
| Fulcrum | Human-AI collaboration — the stable pivot | Contact point, pivot, seam between human judgment and agent execution |

The three brand colors map to these roles. Visuals for the **principles lens** use this metaphor directly and concretely — not as abstract icon, but as mechanical or physical object. Visuals for the **tools lens** encode the metaphor through product structure (DAGs, worktrees, plugin composition). The **workflow capstone** shows the full loop: lever in motion, load being moved, fulcrum holding.

A visual that replaces the metaphor with generic tech imagery (nodes, code text, glowing interfaces) fails the brand-fit criterion regardless of color accuracy.

data.text: Now let me lint the DESIGN.md to see if it passes.
The lint passes with 0 errors (only warnings, which are expected — the brand colors are pre-existing and the unreferenced color tokens are intentional semantic references). Let me commit.

git add DESIGN.md package.json bun.lock && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: author DESIGN.md visual language + add @google/design.md linter [ddx-905c07d0]

Captures DDx's existing rgba triad (lever blue #4878C6, fulcrum green
#35A35F, load purple #8E35A3) as formal design tokens with the
lever/load/fulcrum metaphor system documented in prose. Installs
@google/design.md@0.1.0 as a dev dependency; lint passes with 0 errors.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

data.text:
Commit succeeded. The bead is complete:

- **`bun add --dev @google/design.md@0.1.0`** ran successfully; `package.json` and `bun.lock` updated.
- **`/DESIGN.md`** authored with YAML front matter (colors, typography, rounded, spacing, components) and prose sections (Overview, Colors, Typography, Layout, Components, Do's and Don'ts, Metaphor System).
- Existing rgba triad captured as hex tokens: lever blue `#4878C6`, fulcrum green `#35A35F`, load purple `#8E35A3`.
- **`bunx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md`** passes with 0 errors.
